name: Las Machotas Loop from Silla de Felipe II
name_original: Las Machotas — circular desde la Silla de Felipe II
description: This difficult 5.1-mile (8.2 km) circular hike from Silla de Felipe II offers panoramic views of El Escorial and the Valmayor Reservoir.
region_intro: Hiking around San Lorenzo De El Escorial offers diverse landscapes within the Sierra de Guadarrama mountains. The region is characterized by extensive pine forests, such as Pinar de Abantos, and protected natural spaces like Bosque de la Herrería. Hikers encounter varied terrain, including mountainous paths, wooded trails, and viewpoints overlooking the Valmayor Reservoir. This area provides a blend of natural beauty and historical attractions, making it suitable for various outdoor activities. Best hiking trails around San Lorenzo De El Escorial The most popular hiking route is Treasures of Abantos Trail, a 5.5 miles (8.8 km) trail that takes 2 hours 50 minutes to complete. This moderate route explores the natural features around Monte Abantos. Another top favourite among local hikers is Las Machotas Loop from Silla de Felipe II, a difficult 5.1 miles (8.2 km) path. This trail offers panoramic views from the Silla de Felipe II viewpoint and traverses varied mountainous terrain. Local hikers also love the Hornillo Waterfall and Monte Abantos Loop, a 7.8 miles (12.5 km) trail leading through the Abantos Pine Forest and past the Hornillo Waterfall, often completed in about 4 hours 20 minutes. Hiking around San Lorenzo De El Escorial is defined by the Sierra de Guadarrama mountains, dense pine forests, and significant viewpoints like Silla de Felipe II. The network offers options for easy family walks to more challenging treks. The routes in San Lorenzo De El Escorial are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 400 reviews. More than 2000 hikers have used komoot to explore San Lorenzo De El Escorial's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around San Lorenzo de El Escorial?
A: San Lorenzo de El Escorial offers a wide variety of hiking opportunities, with over 80 trails documented on komoot. These routes cater to all levels, from easy strolls to challenging mountain treks within the Sierra de Guadarrama.

Q: What types of terrain can I expect on the hiking trails?
A: The terrain around San Lorenzo de El Escorial is diverse, ranging from well-maintained paths through dense pine forests like the Pinar de Abantos and the Bosque de la Herrería, to more rugged mountainous trails. You'll encounter varied landscapes, including open viewpoints, wooded sections, and paths near the Valmayor Reservoir.

Q: Are there any easy, family-friendly hiking options?
A: Yes, the region provides several easy routes suitable for families. While specific stroller-friendly paved paths are available in some areas, many trails offer gentle gradients through forests, perfect for a leisurely walk. For example, parts of the Bosque de la Herrería are ideal for a relaxed family outing.

Q: Are there any dog-friendly hiking trails in San Lorenzo de El Escorial?
A: Many trails in the Sierra de Guadarrama, including those around San Lorenzo de El Escorial, are dog-friendly. It's generally recommended to keep dogs on a leash, especially in protected natural areas or where wildlife might be present. Always check local regulations for specific routes.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or viewpoints I can discover while hiking?
A: Hikers can enjoy several significant landmarks and viewpoints. The famous &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/740607" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Silla de Felipe II&lt;/a&gt; offers spectacular panoramic views of the Royal Monastery and the Guadarrama Valley. Other notable spots include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2505177" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mount Abantos&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5155198" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valmayor Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, and the charming &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3121309" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ermita del Cerrillo&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1356986885/hornillo-waterfall-and-monte-abantos-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hornillo Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; is also a beautiful natural feature found on some loops.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes available?
A: Yes, many of the trails around San Lorenzo de El Escorial are designed as circular routes, allowing you to start and end at the same point. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1375168415/las-machotas-loop-from-silla-de-felipe-ii" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Las Machotas Loop from Silla de Felipe II&lt;/a&gt;, which offers varied terrain and stunning views.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in this region?
A: The region is beautiful year-round, but spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant hiking conditions with mild temperatures and vibrant natural colors. Summer can be hot, especially at lower elevations, while winter brings cooler temperatures and the possibility of snow at higher altitudes in the Sierra de Guadarrama.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in San Lorenzo de El Escorial?
A: The hiking trails in San Lorenzo de El Escorial are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 4.6 stars from over 400 reviews. Hikers often praise the diverse landscapes, the well-maintained paths, and the stunning views, particularly from spots like the Silla de Felipe II.

Q: Are there any trails that feature waterfalls?
A: Yes, the area boasts natural features like the Hornillo Waterfall. You can experience this on routes such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1356986885/hornillo-waterfall-and-monte-abantos-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hornillo Waterfall and Monte Abantos Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through the scenic Abantos Pine Forest.

Q: Can I combine hiking with cultural attractions in San Lorenzo de El Escorial?
A: Absolutely. San Lorenzo de El Escorial is unique in its blend of natural beauty and historical significance. Many hiking trails are easily accessible from the town, allowing you to combine your outdoor adventure with a visit to the magnificent &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4318799" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Royal Monastery of San Lorenzo de El Escorial&lt;/a&gt;, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, or explore its surrounding gardens.

Q: Are there any challenging routes for experienced hikers?
A: Yes, for experienced hikers seeking a challenge, there are several difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1375168415/las-machotas-loop-from-silla-de-felipe-ii" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Las Machotas Loop from Silla de Felipe II&lt;/a&gt; is classified as difficult, featuring significant elevation changes and rugged terrain, offering a rewarding experience with panoramic views.

Q: What kind of flora can I expect to see along the trails?
A: The region is rich in diverse flora, thanks to its location in the Sierra de Guadarrama. You'll encounter extensive pine forests, particularly Scots pine in areas like Pinar de Abantos, alongside Pyrenean oak, holm oak, maple, horse chestnut, linden, and cherry trees. The Bosque de la Herrería is also known for its varied plant life.
tips:
From this natural observatory the monarch contemplated the development of the works of the Monastery.
Some experts assure that these granite seats, for the king and two companions, are the remains of a thousand-year-old Celtiberian altar. From here you can perceive that the Monastery has the shape of an inverted grill, in homage to San Lorenzo, who was martyred in one of them back in the year 258, since it is located on a small hill from which the Guadarrama Valley and the Sierra de Navacerrada.

The Chair of Philip II is a famous granite viewpoint in San Lorenzo de El Escorial, near Madrid. Legend has it that King Philip II used it to view the construction of the Monastery of El Escorial.
However, experts believe it was actually an ancient Celtic altar, possibly used for rituals. Today, it's a popular hiking spot with stunning views of the monastery and its surroundings.

This 1,410 meter high summit is one of the two Machotas. Located outside the line of the Guadarrama mountain range, its origin is in a plutonic extrusion that took place millions of years ago. In front of her is her older sister, Machota Alta, separated by the Entre Cabezas hill.

This hill is located right between the Machota Alta and the Baja, and connects the area of the Silla de Felipe II with Zarzalejo. Once at this point, we can climb either of the two Machotas or begin the descent, either to the Felipe II chair or to Zarzalejo. A little further along the road, towards Zarzalejo, we can find the Entre Cabezas fountain.

The stone wall near the road is the rest of the Fence or Royal Wall, built by order of Felipe II, at the end of the 16th century, to mark the limits of the hunting ground of the El Escorial Monastery. Some time later, Carlos IV improved the fence by placing “jumping points” at strategic points, so that game species could enter the preserve but could not leave. The path leaves the highest point of the Machota Alta on the left and heads directly to the group of cliffs where El Fraile is, a granite spire that is shaped like a cappuccino, and also a lit candle, a stone flame. The path continues parallel to the Royal Wall, between cavalry stones and views of the monumental complex of San Lorenzo de El Escorial.

faqs.question: How do I get to the trailhead and where can I park?
faqs.answer: The trailhead for this circular route is at the Silla de Felipe II. It's conveniently accessible by car, train, and bus. There is parking available at the starting point.

faqs.question: Is this trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, this trail is generally dog-friendly. However, it's always recommended to keep dogs on a leash, especially given the varied terrain and potential for wildlife encounters within the natural area.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: The trail can be enjoyed year-round, but spring and autumn offer the most pleasant conditions with milder temperatures and vibrant scenery. Summers can be hot, and parts of the trail are exposed, so an early start is advisable. Winters can bring colder temperatures and potentially slippery conditions on rocky sections.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required for this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to hike this trail, as it is located within the Paraje Pintoresco del Pinar de Abantos y Zona de La Herrería, which is freely accessible to the public.

faqs.question: How difficult is the Las Machotas circular trail?
faqs.answer: This trail is rated as difficult, requiring good physical fitness and firm footing. It involves significant inclines, especially through the Bosque de la Herrería, and some sections have slippery stone ground and rocky granite formations. While some parts might feel simpler to experienced hikers, the full circular route covering both Machotas is a demanding hike.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the trail?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter a diverse landscape, including mountain trails, natural paths, and both unpaved and paved sections. Expect narrow paths, significant inclines, and rocky granite formations, particularly around The Lower Machota. Some areas can be overgrown or muddy depending on the season.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest and views along the route?
faqs.answer: The trail offers continuous panoramic views. You'll start at the historic Seat of Philip II with views of El Escorial. Highlights include the View of El Escorial and the Monastery, the summit of The Lower Machota, and the distinctive El Fraile Rock Formation. You'll also pass through the beautiful Bosque de la Herrería and along sections of the historic Cerca Real (Royal Wall).

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route shares sections with several other paths. Notably, it covers 62% of the 'De Valencia a Lisboa (Etapa desde San Lorenzo del Escorial a Pelayos de la Presa)' trail, and also intersects with 'Camino de las Arenillas', 'Carretera de la Fuente de la Reina', and a small portion of the GR-10.

faqs.question: Can I shorten the route if I find it too challenging?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route offers flexibility. You can choose to ascend only one of the Machotas (either Machota Alta or Machota Baja) to reduce the overall length and challenge of the hike. This allows you to customize the experience to your fitness level.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this hike?
faqs.answer: Given the trail's difficulty and varied terrain, it's essential to bring appropriate footwear with good grip, sufficient water, snacks, sun protection (hat, sunscreen, sunglasses), and layers of clothing as conditions can change. A map or GPS device is also recommended.

faqs.question: Is there shade along the trail?
faqs.answer: The initial ascent through the Bosque de la Herrería offers some shade from chestnut and oak trees. However, the final stretch leading from Entrecabezas Pass to Las Machotas is largely exposed and offers little shade, so be prepared for sun exposure, especially during warmer months.

waypoints.name: View of El Escorial and the Monastery
